title: Min-Heap Insert (sift-up)
code: public void Push(int x)                        // O(log n)

frames.caption: Start with an empty min-heap. We push 5, 3, 8, 1, 4 one at a time, sifting each new value up.
frames.note: A binary heap is a complete tree packed into an array with one invariant: every parent is <= its children (min-heap), so the smallest element is always at the root. Push appends a leaf and restores that invariant with sift-up. The tree on screen is pre-sized to its final shape so each insert simply reveals the next slot.

frames.caption: Push 5: place it at the next free leaf, index 0.
frames.note: Push always starts the same way: append the new value at the first free leaf — the end of the array, which is the next open slot of the complete tree. This keeps the tree COMPLETE (every level full, last level filling left-to-right), which is exactly the property that lets a flat array encode the tree with no pointers: the children of index i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2, and the parent of i is (i-1)/2.

frames.caption: 5 reached the root (i = 0): the new minimum sits on top.
frames.note: Sift-up stops the instant the child is >= its parent (or it reaches the root, i = 0). One root-to-leaf path was repaired and every other parent/child pair was already ordered, so the whole heap satisfies parent <= children again. A priority queue is just this structure with a hidden door: O(1) peek at the smallest (the root) and O(log n) push/pop — the right tool whenever a problem repeatedly asks for "the next smallest/largest."

frames.caption: Push 3: place it at the next free leaf, index 1.
frames.note: Push always starts the same way: append the new value at the first free leaf — the end of the array, which is the next open slot of the complete tree. This keeps the tree COMPLETE (every level full, last level filling left-to-right), which is exactly the property that lets a flat array encode the tree with no pointers: the children of index i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2, and the parent of i is (i-1)/2.

frames.caption: Compare a[1]=3 with parent a[0]=5.
frames.note: Sift-up compares the new node only with its PARENT at (i-1)/2 — never its siblings, which a heap leaves unordered. If the child is smaller than its parent the min-heap invariant (parent <= both children) is violated locally, so they must swap; if not, every ancestor is already <= it and we can stop early.

frames.caption: 3 < parent: swap up, now at index 0.
frames.note: Swap the child up past its larger parent and continue from the parent index. Each swap moves the value one level closer to the root, and a complete tree of n nodes has height floor(log2 n), so sift-up touches at most log n levels — that is why push is O(log n), not O(n). The swap fixes the violation locally while preserving order everywhere else on the path.

frames.caption: 3 reached the root (i = 0): the new minimum sits on top.
frames.note: Sift-up stops the instant the child is >= its parent (or it reaches the root, i = 0). One root-to-leaf path was repaired and every other parent/child pair was already ordered, so the whole heap satisfies parent <= children again. A priority queue is just this structure with a hidden door: O(1) peek at the smallest (the root) and O(log n) push/pop — the right tool whenever a problem repeatedly asks for "the next smallest/largest."

frames.caption: 8 >= 3: 8 is in order, sift-up stops.
frames.note: Sift-up stops the instant the child is >= its parent (or it reaches the root, i = 0). frames.caption: All five pushed. The min-heap is [1, 3, 8, 5, 4] — root 1 is the minimum, in O(1).
frames.note: Each push cost O(log n) sift-up, so building this way is O(n log n) for n separate inserts (Floyd's bottom-up build-heap would be O(n)). The payoff: peek the minimum in O(1) at the root, and pop/push the next extreme in O(log n) forever after. That O(1) peek + O(log n) update is precisely what a priority queue sells — used for top-k, k-way merge, Dijkstra, and "next best" greedy scheduling.
